coredata.dc:title: Controllable synthesis of superparamagnetic NiCo-graphene quantum dot-graphene composite with excellent dispersion for high performance magnetic field-controlled electrochemical flow hybrid supercapacitor
coredata.dc:creator: Keyang, He
Ruiyi, Li
Zaijun, Li
Yongqiang, Yang
coredata.dc:description: The in-situ formation, fixation and structural control of metal nanoparticles on graphene sheets are crucial issue to fabricate metal-graphene composite with high electrochemical performance. The study reports the synthesis of superparamagnetic NiCo-graphene quantum dot-graphene composite (NiCo-GQD-G). Histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were immobilized on graphene sheets of graphite oxide (GO) to form GQD-GO hybrid via π-π stacking. The hybrid are combined with Ni2+ and Co2+ to produce NiCo-GQD-GO complex. Followed by thermal reduction and partial oxidation. The resulting NiCo-GQD-G has a well-defined three-dimensional structure, monodispersity and superparamagnetic. NiCo nanocrystals were covered by NiO and CoO to produce core-shell structure with average particle size of 7.2 nm. The nanostructure can be effectively adjusted by varying the mass ratios of GQD/GO and NiCo/GO. The unique architecture promotes enhanced electron/ion conductivity, catalytic activity and magnetofluid property. The NiCo-GQD-G was adsorbed on nickel foam by magnet to make an electrode without any binder and conductive agent. The electrode exhibits high capacity (1808 F g−1 at 1 A g−1), rate-capacity (1525 F g−1 at 10 A g−1 and 900 F g−1 at 200 A g−1) and cycling stability (99.2% of capacity retention after 5000 cycles at 10 A g−1) in 3.0 M KOH electrolyte. The flow hybrid supercapacitor provides energy density of 247.4 W h kg−1 at power density of 6596 W kg−1 and 111.2 W h kg−1 at power density of 160100 W kg−1. The synthesis can be also used for construction of other metal-graphene composites with excellent dispersion and controllable nanostructure.

The charge-discharge curves (A) of flow supercapacitor at current densities of 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8 and 10 A g−1 (from right to left) in 3 M KOH aqueous electrolyte and the specific capacity (B) of flow hybrid supercapacitor at different current densities.
Ragone plot of NiCo-GQD-G electrode in 3 M KOH aqueous electrolyte using a flow hybrid supercapacitor.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The in-situ formation, fixation and structural control of metal nanoparticles on graphene sheets are crucial issue to fabricate metal-graphene composite with high electrochemical performance. The study reports the synthesis of superparamagnetic NiCo-graphene quantum dot-graphene composite (NiCo-GQD-G). Histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were immobilized on graphene sheets of graphite oxide (GO) to form GQD-GO hybrid via π-π stacking. The hybrid are combined with Ni2+ and Co2+ to produce NiCo-GQD-GO complex. Followed by thermal reduction and partial oxidation. The resulting NiCo-GQD-G has a well-defined three-dimensional structure, monodispersity and superparamagnetic. NiCo nanocrystals were covered by NiO and CoO to produce core-shell structure with average particle size of 7.2 nm. The nanostructure can be effectively adjusted by varying the mass ratios of GQD/GO and NiCo/GO. The unique architecture promotes enhanced electron/ion conductivity, catalytic activity and magnetofluid property. The NiCo-GQD-G was adsorbed on nickel foam by magnet to make an electrode without any binder and conductive agent. The electrode exhibits high capacity (1808 F g−1 at 1 A g−1), rate-capacity (1525 F g−1 at 10 A g−1 and 900 F g−1 at 200 A g−1) and cycling stability (99.2% of capacity retention after 5000 cycles at 10 A g−1) in 3.0 M KOH electrolyte. The flow hybrid supercapacitor provides energy density of 247.4 W h kg−1 at power density of 6596 W kg−1 and 111.2 W h kg−1 at power density of 160100 W kg−1. The synthesis can be also used for construction of other metal-graphene composites with excellent dispersion and controllable nanostructure.

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitor is one energy storage device between traditional capacitor and rechargeable battery. It not only has the characteristics of rapid charging and discharging of capacitor, but also has the characteristics of energy storage of battery [1]. Because of high power density, fast charge and discharge rate and long cycle life, supercapacitors have been widely applied in automotive, heavy transportations, renewable energy, backup energy, wireless communication, consumer electronics, satellite and spacecraft radiation resistant microelectronic components and systems [2]. However, low energy density limits its applications in some fields requiring a higher energy [3]. Development of battery-type electrode materials for hybrid supercapacitor is key to solve the problem [4].

The current electrode materials for supercapacitors are various carbon materials, such as activated carbon [5], carbon nanotubes [6], graphene [7] and graphene quantum dots [8], and transition metal nanomaterials [9]. Graphene as electrode material has attracted a more attention, due to high electrical conductivity, large surface and chemical stability [10]. However, graphene is not electroactive material that only produces double electric layer capacitance (DELC). The specific capacitance is mostly lower than 350 F g−1, which is close to the capacitance of activated carbon [6]. Transition metal nanomaterials are the most commonly used electroactive materials in industry, owing to low price, stable structure and rich resource. However, its poor conductivity limits the applications in hybrid supercapacitor. To realize complementary advantages of graphene and transition metal nanomaterial, many metal nanomaterials were integrated with graphene to fabricate metal-graphene composite for hybrid supercapacitor, including Co2SiO4 [11], Co3O4 [12], CoMn2O4 [13], Co(OH)2/Au-Ag [14], Co3O4–NiO [15], MnO2 [16], MnCo2S4 [17], MoO3 [18], NiCo-LDH [19], NiCoAl-LDH [20], NiO@Co3O4 [21], NiOOH [22], V2O5/Fe3O4 [23] and VO2 [24]. The metal-graphene composites can offer an improved electrochemical property. However, the improvement depends on nanostructure of metal nanomaterial and its dispersion on graphene sheets.

Several strategies have been developed for the synthesis of metal-graphene composite to improve the electrochemical properties. The first strategy is called as the layer-by-layer. It builds metal-graphene composite by alternately depositing graphene and metal nanomaterial [25]. Peng et al. developed one spin-coating method to make the percolating film composing of graphene and silver nanowires [26]. Firstly, spin coating silver nanowires on polyethylene terephthalate film formed the silver nanowire network. Then, spin coating graphene oxide aqueous solution on the silver nanowire network formed the graphene layer. The above procedure were repeated until a certain number of cycles. The resulting Ag-graphene film has been used as the electrode for transparent and flexible supercapacitor. Omer Sadak et al. reported one electrodeposition method for the construction of MnO2-graphene composite [27]. In one-step simple electrodeposition, the formation and fixation of MnO2 nanoflowers on graphene paper were achieved. The as-synthesized MnO2-graphene electrode for supercapacitor offers high capacitance of 76.8 mF cm−1 at 0.05 mA cm−2. The layer-by-layer strategy can effectively reduce the agglomeration of metal nanomaterial and graphene sheets, due to the barrier effect of metal nanomaterial and graphene. Because metal nanoparticles and graphene sheets lack of sufficient contact, the combination of metal nanomaterial and graphene is difficult to produce significant electrochemical synergy. The second strategy is called as the co-precipitation method. It makes metal-graphene composite via one-step hydrothermal treatment of mixing metal salt aqueous solution with graphene oxide [28]. Naz et al. reported the synthesis of MoS2-graphene composite by hydrothermal reaction of mixed hexaammonium molybdate tetrahydrate aqueous solution with graphite oxide and thiourea [29]. One step hydrothermal reaction achieves to the reduction of graphene oxide and the formation of MoS2 nanosheets. The resulted composite exhibits a well-defined three dimensional structure, in which MoS2 nanosheets are well dispersed on the graphene networks. High water-solubility makes transition metal salt and graphite oxide easy to dissolve in water to form stable dispersion. The characteristic helps to improve the dispersion of metal-graphene composite. However, the functional groups of graphene oxide such as hydroxyl and carboxyl are mainly distributed on the edges of graphene sheets. The interaction between the functional groups of graphene oxide and the metal ions makes the most of metal nanoparticles are scattered at the edges of graphene sheets and rarely at the surface of graphene sheets, resulting a poor dispersion in the final product. The third strategy is called as the two-step method. The metal nanoparticles or precursors were prepared and then added into graphene oxide aqueous solution to fabricate greaphene aerogel via hydrothermal treatment. The metal nanoparticles were embedded by graphene sheets with the reduction of graphene oxide. Because of the lack of strong interaction force between metal nanomaterial and graphene, the agglomeration of metal nanomaterial and graphene is difficult to be effectively avoided during hydrothermal treatment. Different from graphene oxide, graphene quantum dots as new member of graphene family are composed of tiny graphene sheets with the rich of functional groups [30]. The structural characteristic makes graphene quantum dots can combine with metal ions to form stable complex. Zhang et al. reported the synthesis of graphene quantum dots/MnCo2O4.5 composite for supercapacitors via hydrothermal treatment of a mixed aqueous solution containing Mn(NO3)2, Co(NO3)2, urea and graphene quantum dots. Because graphene quantum dots induce the growth of nanostructured MnCo2O4.5 composites, the composites with different nanostructures were obtained by varying the amounts of graphene quantum dots [31]. However, the introduction of graphene quantum dots cannot significantly improve the electronic conductivity of metal-graphene composite because it is a typical semiconductor material. Based on different functions of graphene oxide and graphene quantum dots in construction of metal-graphene composite, we believe that the integration of graphene with graphene quantum dots can realize controllable synthesis of metal-graphene composite with excellent dispersion. As far as I know, no relevant research was reported up to now.

In the study, we report the synthesis of NiCo-graphene quantum dot-graphene composite (NiCo-GQD-G). The use of histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots achieves to in-situ formation, fixation and structural control of NiCo nanoparticles on graphene sheets. The as-synthesized NiCo-GQD-G shows high electron/ion conductivity, catalytic activity and superparamagnetic. The NiCo-GQD-G as a battery-type electrode material for magnetic field-controlled flow hybrid supercapacitor provides excellent electrochemical performances.

Citric acid, histidine, nickel chloride and cobalt chloride were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Company (www.sigmaaldrich.com/china-mainland.html). Graphite oxide (GO) was prepared from natural graphite using the Hummers’ method [32]. Histidine-functional graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was prepared by using a reported method in Ref. [33]. All other reagents employed were of analytical reagent grade or with highest quality and were purchased from Shanghai Chemical Company (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water (18.2 MU cm) purified from a Milli-Q purification system was used throughout the experiment.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) analysis was carried out in HITACHI S4800 field emission scanning electron microscope. SEM sample was prepared by placing a drop of dilute ethanol dispersion of material onto a copper plate attached to one aluminum sample holder, and the solvent was allowed to evaporate at room temperature. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were conducted on a JEOL 2010 transmission electron microscope at 200 keV. The sample was prepared by dispensing a small amount of dry powder in ethanol. Then, one drop of the suspension was dropped on 300 mesh copper. The TEM grid covered with thin amorphous carbon film. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured on X-ray D8 Advance Instrument operated at 40 kV and 20 mA and using Cu Kα radiation source with λ = 0.15406 nm. The N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms were measured at 77 K on a Quantachrome Nova 2000. Prior to gas sorption measurements, all the samples were outgassed in vacuum at 120 °C for 24 h. Raman measurements were carried out using InVia laser micro-Raman spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed using a PHI 5700 ESCA spectrometer with monochromated Al KR radiation. The mass loading of NiCo in NiCo-GQD-G composite was determined by atomic absorption spectrometry after NiCo was changed into Ni2+ and Co2+ using a 5 mol L−1 nitric acid.

The mixed histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dot solution (300 mg in 50 mL of ultrapure water) with the graphene oxide dispersion (240 mg in 50 mL of ultrapure water). After adjusted the acidity to pH 7 by adding 1 M NaOH aqueous solution, slowly dropped 12 mL of the mixed solution containing 0.1 M NiCl2 and 0.1 M CoCl2 under strongly agitation. The collected precipitate was dried at 80 °C for 4 h, reduced at 600 °C in N2 atmosphere for 2 h and oxidized at 100 °C in air for 1 h in sequence to obtain NiCo-GQD-G. The above procedure was used for synthesis of NiCo-GQD unless no addition of graphene oxide. A similar method reported in reference was used for preparation of NiCo [34]. In a typical synthesis, 20 mL of 0.1 M NaOH aqueous solution was dropped into 12 mL of the mixed aqueous solution containing 0.1 M NiCl2 and 0.1 M CoCl2 with stirring. The collected precipitate was annealed at 500 °C in H2 gas flow for 4 h and then oxidized in the air at 100 °C for 1 h to obtain NiCo-GQD-G.

One three-electrode test system was used for studying on electrochemical properties of NiCo-GQD-G in 3 M KOH aqueous solution electrolyte. In the three-electrode system, the nickel foam bearing electroactive material was used as a working electrode, and platinum foil (1 cm × 1 cm) and saturated calomel electrode were used as the counter electrode and reference electrode. The working electrode was prepared by following procedure: one nickel foam flake (1 cm × 1 cm) was firstly pre-treated and pressed at 100 MPa for 5 min [35]. The treated nickel foam flake and one permanent magnet flake ((1 cm × 1 cm) were bound together by adhesion with EVA adhesive. The amounts of electroactive material were adsorbed on the surface of nickel foam through magnetic attraction between material and magnet.

One flow hybrid supercapacitor with equivalent quantities of electrolyte at both sides of the cell was used for evaluating electrochemical performances of NiCo-GQD-G (Fig. 1 ) [36]. The electrode is the magnetic fluid composing of NiCo-GQD-G and 3 M KOH aqueous solution (1.0 mg mL−1). One cellulose filter paper is used as the separator. When the charging is required, the magnetic fluid in the container is pumped into an electrochemical flow cell. At the same time, turns on electromagnet to adsorb NiCo-GQD-G in the magnetic fluid on the surface of nickel foam. After the charging, turn off electromagnet to remove NiCo-GQD-G on the nickel foam. The NiCo-GQD-G will enter into the fluid. Followed by pumping into another container. The above procedure is also used for the discharging unless the charged magnetic fluid instead of the magnetic fluid.

The in-situ formation, fixation and structural control of metal nanomaterials on graphene sheets are crucial issue to fabricate metal-graphene composite with excellent electrochemical performance. Herein, we designed one strategy for the synthesis of NiCo-GQD-G. Fig. 2 shows that the synthesis mainly includes three processes. The first process is to bind histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) on the graphene sheets of graphite oxide (GO) to form GQD-GO hybrid. The GQDs are composed of tiny graphene sheets with several nanometer size and the rich of functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, amido and imidazole groups [37]. The structural characteristics make the GQDs have a high water-solubility. This is beneficial to improve the contact between the GO and the GQD in an aqueous medium. By the π-π stacking interaction between graphene sheets of GO and GQD and imidazole rings, the GQDs can be dispersed on the graphene sheets of GO to produce stable GQD-GO hybrid. Moreover, the GQDs are also bound with GO by Van Der Waals and hydrogen bond between different hydrophilic groups of GO and GQD. As the GQDs contain a large number of imidazole rings at the edges of graphene sheets that can combine with many metal ions to form stable complex via coordination bond, the integration of GO with GQD creates abundant sites for binding metal ions. The above suggest has been confirmed by monitoring the fluorescent behavior in the mixing process of GQD with GO. Fig. s1 presents the change in the fluorescence emission of GQD with increase of GO concentration. The fluorescence intensity of GQD rapidly decreases with increasing GO concentration, verifying that a strong interaction occurs in between GQD and GO. The interaction achieves to the energy transfer from GQD to GO and leads to a rapid fluorescence quenching. When the amounts of GO is more than 240 mg, the fluorescence quenching rate reaches the maximum. Continue to increase GO concentration can not cause an obvious change in the fluorescence emission. The result demonstrates that the GQDs bound on the graphene sheets of GO reaches the maximum. To avoid the existence of free GQDs in the system, the GO of 240 mg was adopted for the synthesis of NiCo-GQD-G. Under the condition, all GQDs in the reaction are firmly immobilized on the surface of large graphene sheets from GO.

The second process is to fix Ni2+ and Co2+ on the surface of graphene sheets. In the process, the mixed NiCl2 aqueous solution with CoCl2 was slowly dropped into the GQD-GO aqueous solution to rapidly form NiCo-GQD-GO complex. This achieves to the immobilization of Ni2+ and Co2+ on the graphene sheets of GO, finally leading to the in-situ formation and fixation of NiCo alloy nanoparticles on the surface of graphene sheets. Based on the theory of coordination chemistry, one Ni2+ (or Co2+) can simultaneously coordinate with four nitrogen atoms in the imidazole groups to form corresponding complex. As a sequence, the coordination causes the polymerization of graphene sheets [38]. With the increase of polymerization degree, the water-solubility rapidly reduces. Hence, an obvious precipitation process was observed when the mixed NiCl2 solution with CoCl2 was added into the GQD-GO hybrid solution. More importantly, the polymerization of graphene sheets brings the formation of three-dimensional graphene networks composing of interwoven graphene sheets in the final product. The three-dimensional structure helps to improve the electrolyte transport during the charge/discharge process.

The third process is to convert NiCo-GQD-GO complex into NiCo-GQD-G composite. Firstly, the complex was subjected to thermal reduction in N2 atmosphere. Under the heating, the function groups in both GO and GQD will be mostly stripped. At the same time, Ni2+ and Co2+ in the complex are converted into Ni0 and Co0, finally leading to the formation of NiCo nanocrystals. The investigation demonstrated that the choice of reduction temperature is important to realize an excellent dispersion of NiCo nanoparticles on the surface of graphene sheets. The use of a relatively high temperature is beneficial to the reduction of GO, GQD and metal ions as well as the formation of NiCo nanoparticles with a high crystallinity. However, the decomposition of GQD and GO produces large amount of gases under a high temperature. The escape of these gases may strip the NiCo nanoparticles from the graphene sheets and moves them in the direction of the airflow, leading to a poor dispersion of NiCo nanoparticles in the final hybrid. To resolve the problem, the temperature of 600 °C was adopted for the reduction of NiCo-GQD-GO. As the meteal oxide offers a higher structural stability, wider electrochemical window and lower electrical conductivity compared to metal, the NiCo nanoparticles were partly oxidized in air at 100 °C for 1 h. In the formed composite, the NiCo alloy nanoparticles were covered by NiO and CoO to form one core-shell nanostructure. Such a special structure makes the composite high electrical conductivity, structural stability and hybrid supercapacitor performance.

To optimize the mass ratio of NiCo, GQD and GO, a series of NiCo-GQD-G composites were synthesized by varying mass ratio of GQD/GO or NiCo/GO. Fig. S2 shows the SEM images of NiCo-GQD-G composites prepared by using 50% of NiCo/GO and different mass ratio of GQD/GO. When the mass ratio of GQD/GO is 10%, the amounts of Ni2+ and Co2+ immobilized on the graphene sheets is very low. As a result, only a few NiCo nanoparticles with small particle size can be formed. With increasing GQD, the amounts of Ni2+ and Co2+ fixed on the graphene sheets will rapidly increase. This results in the formation of more NiCo nanoparticles with bigger particle size. Thus, more and more dense NiCo nanoparticles appear in the composite. Fig. S2 also indicates a well-defined three-dimensional graphene network in the composite when the mass ratio of GQD/GO does not exceed 125%. This is due to the coordination between Ni2+ (or Co2+) and imidazole groups in the GQD-GO hybrids, which are distributed on different graphene sheets of GO. The three-dimensional graphene networks helps to the improvement of electron/ion conductivity during the charge/discharge process for supercapacitors. Fig. s3 shows the SEM images of NiCo-GQD-G composites prepared by using 125% of GQD/GO and different mass ratio of NiCo/GO. When the mass ratio of NiCo/GO is less than 1.0%, no precipitation can be observed on the SEM image. This is because the amounts of Ni2+ and Co2+ immobilized on the graphene sheets is very small. As a result, only small number of NiCo nanoparticles with tiny particle size were produced. These NiCo nanoparticles offer high water-solubility and can be well dispersed in the aqueous solution. Thus, no precipitation was separated from the system. Only a few NiCo nanoparticles appear on the graphene sheets. With increasing NiCo/GO, more NiCo nanoparticles with bigger particle size were produced. The amounts of Ni2+ and Co2+ fixed on the graphene sheets will rapidly increase with increasing GQD concentration. A higher concentration of GQD results a more Ni2+ and Co2+ were immobilized on the graphene sheets. As a consequence, more dense NiCo nanoparticles appear on the surface of graphene sheets. When the mass ratio of NiCo/GO changes over a wide range, the NiCo nanoparticles in the composite display a regular spherical structure with similar particle size. These NiCo nanoparticles are evenly distributed on the graphene sheets, indicating excellent dispersion. However, some NiCo nanorods appear in the NiCo-GQD-G composite when the mass ratio of NiCo/GO is more than 50%. In the presence of high concentration of Ni2+ and Co2+, there are a fast growth of NiCo crystals during the thermal reduction. The GQDs induce the growth of NiCo nanocrystals and leads to produce the NiCo nanoparticles with different morphology. Different crystal surface of NiCo nanoparticles may offer a different adsorption capacity towards the GQDs. This induces a different growth rates for different crystal surfaces and finally leads to the formation of NiCo nanorods. The regulating function of GQDs has been confirmed in the synthesis of other metal-graphene composites [14]. The above results indicate that our synthesis can achieve to effectively control the size, density and structure of NiCo nanoparticles in the final NiCo-GQD-G composite via varying the mass ratios of GQD/GO and NiCo/GO.

Fig. 3 shows SEM, TEM, High resolution TEM (HRTEM) images, elemental marppings of the as-synthesized NiCo-GQD-G. The SEM and TEM analysis reveal that the NiCo-GQD-G offers well-defined three-dimensional structure. The small NiCo nanoparticles with the average particle size of 7.2 nm are well distributed on the graphene networks, indicating excellent dispersion. To understand the roles of GQDs in the synthesis of NiCo-GQD-G, two kinds of NiCo-graphene composites were prepared by using the same procedure unless no addition of GQDs (NiCo-G-1) or replacing GQDs with CTAB (NiCo-G-2). Fig. s4 shows the SEM images of NiCo-G-1 and NiCo-G-2. It can be seen from Fig. s4A that all NiCo nanoparticles are scattered around the edges of graphene sheets. Different graphene, GO contains some function groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl at the edges of graphene sheets. These groups can combine with Ni2+ and Co2+ and leads to the fixation of NiCo nanoparticles at the edges of graphene sheets. As there are rare functional groups on the surface of graphene sheet, no NiCo nanoparticles appear on the surface of graphene sheets. Fig. s4A also indicates that the NiCo nanoparticles in NiCo-G-1 offer the particle size of about 150 nm. The size is remarkably bigger than that in the NiCo-GQD-G, verifying that the synthesis tends to the formation of a relatively big NiCo nanoparticles in the absence of GQDs. Fig. s4B shows that there is serious agglomeration of NiCo nanoparticles in NiCo-G-2. In the presence of CTAB, the combination of negative charge GO with positive charge CTAB by their electrostatic attraction loses the ability of function groups in GO to fix NiCo nanoparticles. Free NiCo nanoparticles. The formed NiCo nanoparticles freely move and cause a serious agglomeration during synthesis of NiCo-G-2. The above results demonstrate that the GQDs play important roles in the fixation of NiCo nanocrystals on the graphene sheets as well as the structural regulation. Further, HRTEM analysis reveals that the NiCo nanoparticles in NiCo-GQD-G has a typical core-shell structure (Fig. 2C). The NiCo alloy (core) is in the center and NiO and CoO (shell) are around the NiCo alloy. The diameter of core is about 3 nm and the shell thickness of shell is about 2 nm. On the HRTEM image there are the lattice spacings of 0.18 and 0.23 nm. These lattice spacings are corresponded to the (110) plane of NiCo alloy nanocrystals and (111) plane of NiO (CoO). The elemental mappings indicate the spatial distributions of nickel (Ni), cobalt (Co) and nitrogen (N) in NiCo-GQD-G surface. All elements have an uniform distribution, verifying that these elements were involved in the formation and crystal growth of NiCo nanoparticles. The result of EDS analysis in Fig. s5 indicates that the NiCo-GQD-G is composed of Ni, Co, C, N and O. As O only come from NiO and CoO and N only come from the GQDs, indicating the existence of NiO, CoO and GQD in NiCo-GQD-G.

Fig. s6 shows the total, C1s, O1s, N1s, Ni2p and Co2p XPS spectra of NiCo-GQD-G. On the total XPS spectrum there are five peaks, corresponding to carbon (C), oxygen (O), nitrogen (N), nickel (Ni) and cobalt (Co). The result demonstrates that NiCo-GQD-G is composed of C, O, N, Ni and Co elements. On the C1s XPS spectrum there are four peaks. The peaks at 284.7, 286.6 and 288.1 eV can be assigned to C–C, C–O and CO species, respectively. The peak at 288.9 eV can be assigned to O–CO. on the N1s XPS spectrum there are three peaks. The peak at 398.4 eV can be assigned to N2, corresponding to pyridinic N. The peak at 399.8 eV can be assigned to amide, amine or pyrrolic N. The peak at 401 eV can be assigned to N4 and corresponds to graphitic N, indicating that nitrogen replaced carbon in graphene sheet and was incorporated into the carbon network. Furthermore, the XPS spectrum also shows that the peak intensity of graphitic nitrogen is stronger than that of pyridinic nitrogen and pyrrolic/pyridinic nitrogen, verifying that graphitic nitrogen is dominant in NiCo-GQD-G. On the O1s XPS spectrum there are two peaks at 529.2 eV and 530.7 eV, belonging to O2− and –OH species. On the Ni2p XPS spectrum there are six peaks. The dual states of Ni3+ appear at 854.6 eV and 871 eV, and the peaks at 856.1 eV and 873.3 eV belong to Ni2+, indicating the existence of NiO. Two peaks at about 855 eV can be assigned to Ni0. On the Co2p XPS spectrum there are six peaks. Two peaks at about 780 eV can be assigned to Co0. The peaks at 779.4 eV and 793.8 eV can be assigned to Co3+, while Co2+ corresponds to 782.0 eV and 796.1 eV.

Fig. 4 shows the XRD patterns, Raman spectrum and magnetic hysteresis curves of NiCo-GQD-G. On the XRD pattern there are eight diffraction peaks at 26°, 32.2°, 37.1°, 43.3°, 44.4°, 51.7°, 60.5° and 76.1°. The peak at 26° is corresponded to (002) plane of graphene. Due to a poor crystallinity, the diffraction peak of graphene displays a wide and weak feature. The peaks at 44.4°, 51.7°and 76.1° are corresponded to the (110), (200) and (220) planes of NiCo alloy (JCPDS card No. 04–0805 and 01–1259). These peaks are sharp and strong, verifying a high crystallinity. The peaks at 32.2° and 60.5° are corresponded to the (111) and (220) planes of CoO (JCPDS card No. 48–1719) and the peaks at 37.1° and 43.3° are assigned to the (101) and (012) planes of NiO (JCPDS card No. 44–1159). On the Raman spectrum there are one wide Raman band and two strong Raman peaks. The band at between 100 and 800 cm−1 can be assigned to the vibrational modes of NiO and CoO [39]. The peaks at 1311 and 1485 cm−1 are corresponded to D, breathing mode of A1g symmetry, and G of graphene, corresponding to E2g symmetry of sp2 carbon atoms, respectively. The hysteresis loop of NiCo-GQD-G is S-shaped curve that passes through the coordinate origin. The result proves that the sample has a negligible coercivity (Hc) and remanence. The above result also verifies that the NiCo-GQD-G is a typical superparamagnetic material [40]. This is attributed to tiny particle size of NiCo nanoparticles.

The contents of Ni and Co in NiCo-GQD-G were determined by atomic absorption spectrometry after digested with concentrated nitric acid. The result shows that the contents of Ni and Co are 21.17% and 20.87% in the composite, respectively. The total content of NiCo reaches to 42.04%. As Ni and Co is high electroactive materials, the above result demonstrates that the composite has a high electrochemical activity as the electrode material for supercapacitors. Further, the electrical conductivities of NiCo-GQD-G, NiCo-GQD and NiCo were measured by using a four-point probe method at 20 MPa. The results were shown in Fig. s7. The electrical conductivity of NiCo-GQD-G reaches 530.8 S m−1. The value is more than 10-fold that of NiCo-GQD (41.37 S m−1), indicating that the introduction of graphene can obviously improve the electrical conductivity. In comparison to NiCo (40.93 S m−1), the NiCo-GQD offers a slight increased electrical conductivity. As the GQD is a typical semiconductor material, the introduction has little effect on improving the conductivity of NiCo. In addition, we also noted that the electrical conductivity of NiCo is much higher than that of NiO and CoO [41]. The result shows the core-shell structure of NiCo nanoparticles brings an obviously enhanced electrical conductivity.

To investigate the role of GQD and graphene in the improvement of electrochemical property, the CV behavior of three electrodes, including NiCo, NiCo-GQD and NiCo-GQD-G, was studied at the scan rate of 50 mV s−1 in a 3 M KOH aqueous solution. Fig. 5 A shows that all CV curves have one pair of redox peaks, because the transformation between different oxidation states of Ni and Co cause a series of Faraday electrode reactions. Compared to NiCo electrode, the NiCo-GQD electrode gives a higher CV current. This is due to special properties of the GQD. As the GQD contains imidazole groups that can react with Ni2+ and Co2+, it can effectively adjust the size of NiCo nanoparticles. As a result, the formed NiCo-GQD has a small particle size with a large specific surface area. The structural feature allows NiCo-GQD to have more electroactive components to be exposed. The GQD has many nitrogen atoms and hydrophilic groups. Their existence greatly improves the hydrophilicity of NiCo-GQD. Thus, there is the strong affinity between the electrode material and the polar electrolyte. The GQD is a kind of N-type semiconductor and while NiO and CoO are P-type semiconductor [42]. The combination of GQD with NiO and CoO leads to the formation of many structures of similar PN-junction on the interfaces. These structures result in a significantly electrocatalytic action towards the redox of NiO and CoO. The above factors bring accelerated electrode and increased CV current. The NiCo-GQD-G has the highest CV current among three electrodes. This is because there is a three-dimensional graphene framework network in the NiCo-GQD-G composite. The graphene network will improve the electron/ion conductivity, leading to an increase the CV current. Further, the CV curves of NiCo-GQD-G electrode at different scan rates were shown in Fig. 5B. The CV current rapidly increases with the increase of scan rate, verifying that the NiCo-GQD-G is of high electronic conductivity.

Fig. 6 shows the charge and discharge curves and specific capacities of NiCo electrode, NiCo-GQD electrode and NiCo-GQD-G electrode in 3 M KOH aqueous solution at the current density of 5 A g−1 using the three-electrodes test system. The each of discharge curves includes three voltage stages. Two stages in the potential range of 0.5–0.28 V and 0.20–0.0 V show a fast potential decrease process. The capacity at the stage come from the electrical double-layer capacity (EDLC). In comparison to NiCo electrode, NiCo-GQD electrode and NiCo-GQD-G electrode display a much higher EDLC. On the one hand, both GQDs and graphene are of large specific surface area, their introduction increases the surface and leads to an increased EDLC. On the other hand, the regulation of GQDs on the nanostructure results in the formation of small NiCo nanoparticles. The small particle size brings a large surface area, leading to an increased EDLC. In addition, the introduction of graphene effectively avoids the agglomeration of NiCo nanoparticles during the synthesis of NiCo-GQD-G. This greatly reduce the loss of surface area caused by the nanoparticle agglomeration. Thus, the NiCo-GQD-G electrode gives the highest EDLC. One stage in the potential range of 0.28–0.20 V indicates a very slow potential decay process. The stage is seriously deviated from straight line. The result proves that the capacities at the stage are come from the Faradaic redox reactions caused by the transformation between different oxidation states of Ni and Co in electrode materials. For all electrodes, the discharge time in the potential range of 0.28–0.20 V is much longer than that in other stages. The result verifies that the capacities of all electrodes are mainly come from the pseudo capacity. Further, the specific capacity of NiCo, NiCo-GQD and NiCo-GQD-G electrodes were calculated from corresponding discharge cures. The results in Fig. 6B indicate that the capacity at 5 A g−1 is 361 F g−1 for NiCo electrode, 952 F g−1 for NiCo-GQD electrode and 1612 F g−1 for NiCo-GQD-G electrode. These data indicate the NiCo-GQD electrode has a much higher capacity compared with NiCo electrode. Its capacity is more than 2-fold that of NiCo-GQD electrode, verifying that the introduction of GQD brings a remarkably enhanced the capacity. This is because the introduction of GQDs increases the surface area and reduces the size of NiCo nanoparticles. The former mainly increases the EDLC and the latter leads to an enhanced pseudo capacity. The small particle size makes more electroactive substances in the NiCo-GQD can be exposed outside and produce a more pseudo capacity. Among three electrodes, the NiCo-GQD-G electrode provides the highest capacitance. This is attributed to three-dimensional structure composed of interweaved graphene sheets. The graphene network can effectively avoid the agglomeration of NiCo nanoparticles during the synthesis. This further increase the exposure degree of electroactive substances and brings an increased pseudo capacity. For NiCo-GQD-G, its capacity mainly come from the pseudo capacity produced by NiO and CoO. The capacity calculated by total mass of nickel and cobalt in the composite is about 3834 F g−1. The value is obviously higher that of the reported NiO/Ni [43] and CoO/Co [34]. The result demonstrates that the use of GQD and graphene for fabrication of metal-graphene composite can bring obviously increased capacity.

The NiCo nanoparticles play important role to produce pseudo capacity for NiCo-GQD-G electrode. To examine the effect of NiCo content in the composite on the supercapacitor performance, a series of NiCo-GQD-G samples were prepared by varying the NiCo/GO mass ratio. Their charge and discharge curves were measured and the results were shown in Fig. 7
. When the mass ratio of NiCO/GO is zero, the discharge curve shows a fast potential decrease process between 0 V and 0.5 V. The result verifies the GQD-G only produce EDLC, indicating that no redox reaction carries out in the GQD and the G during the discharge process. When the mass ratio of NiCo/GO increase to 10%, the discharge curve appears a slow potential decrease process between 0.28 V and 0.20. This is attributed to the pseudo capacity produced by NiCo nanoparticles in the composite. With the increase of NiCo, the specific capacity rapidly increases when the ratio is less than 40%. However, the specific capacity slightly reduces with the increase of NiCo when the ratio is more than 40%. This is because high concentration of Ni2+ and Co2+ results in the formation of more and bigger NiCo nanoparticles. These NiCo nanoparticles may agglomerate and cause the loss of surface area. This brings a reduced specific capacity.

Fig. 8A presents the charge/discharge curves of NiCo-GQD-G electrode at the current density in the range from in 3 M KOH aqueous solution. The discharge capacity reaches to 1808 F g−1 at the current density of 1 A g−1. With the increase of current density the discharge capacity will slowly reduce. This is because the discharge process has a higher rate compared with the electrode reaction. Further, Fig. 8B indicates the relationship curve of discharge capacity with the current density. It can be seen from Fig. 8B that the capacity of NiCo-GQD-G electrode gives a good capacity retention. The capacity can remain 1248 F g−1 at current density of 50 A g−1 and 900 F g−1 at the current density of 200 A g−1, indicating a good high-rate performance. Fig. 8B also indicates that the high-rate performance of NiCo-GQD-G electrode is much better that of NiCo electrode and NiCo-GQD electrode. We suggest that such a good high-rate performance is due to well-defined three-dimensional graphene network, small particle size and core-shell structure of NiCo nanoparticles and hybrid of graphene, GQD and NiCoO. The three-dimensional structure offers a high electron/ion conductivity, acts as an ion reservoir to reduce the ion diffusion distance of electrolyte ions to the interior of NiCo-GQD-G surfaces and work as a framework for NiCo nanoparticles. More importantly, the combination of graphene network with NiCo nanoparticles largely increases the specific surface area and the electronic conductivity. The small particle size makes NiCo nanoparticles have large specific surface area, short distance for electron/ion transfer during the discharge process and full exposure of electroactive substances. The core-shell structure achieves to the combination of high electron conductivity of NiCo and high capacity of NiO and CoO. In addition, the core-shell structure largely improve the structural stability of NiCo nanomaterial during the discharge process. The hybrid of graphene, GQD and NiCoO crate special structure of similar PN-junction on the interface. These special structure can catalyze the redox process and leads to a faster electrode reaction. Therefore, the NiCo-GQD-G electrode achieves to an excellent high-rate performance.

The cycling performance of NiCo-GQD-G electrode was evaluated by continuous galvanostatic charge and discharge 5000 cycles at the current density of 10 A g−1. Fig. 9 A presents the charge and discharge curves of 20-cycle. The each cycling curve has a similar potential-time response. This demonstrates that the electrode reactions are quasi-reversible electrochemical process. Based on data in Fig. 9B, the capacity retention rate of NiCo-GQD-G electrode was calculated. It was found that the capacity retention rate reaches to 99.2% with the Coulombic efficiency of 99.8–100.2% after 5000 cycling, indicating good cycle stability. We suggest that the high cycling performance is due to the graphene network and core-shell structure. Due to excellent mechanical properties, graphene network can release the stress produced in the process of charging and discharging. This helps to improve the structural stability of electrodes during the charge and discharge process. In comparison to metal crystals, metal oxide offers a hgiher structural stability. Thus, the core-shell structure greatly enhances the stability of NiCo nanoparticles. The above factors leads to an obviously improved cyling stability of NiCo-GQD-G electrode.

The as-synthesized NiCo-GQD-G is easily dispersed in an aqueous medium to form a stable dispersion. If a magnetic field is applied, NiCo-GQD-G in the dispersion rapidly move in the magnetic direction (Fig. s8). Excellent magnetic fluid properties make NiCo-GQD-G suitable for magnetic field-controlled electrochemical flow hybrid supercapacitor. To evaluate the NiCo-GQD-G electrode for practical application, one flow hybrid supercapacitor device was assembled (Fig. 1). The flow hybrid supercapacitor uses NiCo-GQD-G as the electrode, 3 M KOH aqueous solution as the electrolyte and cellulose paper as the separator. Its electrochemical performances were studied on the electrochemical workstation. The CV curves of the NiCo-GQD-G electrode in 3 M KOH aqueous solution using the flow hybrid supercapacitor was shown in Fig. 10 . There is one pair of redox peaks on the CV curve, which are attributed to the oxidation and reduction of NiCo-GQD-G at the scan rate of 10 mV s−1 in the potential range from 0 to 2 V. The voltage window of the flow hybrid supercapacitor reaches to 1.65 V, which is much bigger than that of the NiCo-GQD-G electrode in the three-electrode test synthesis. The wide voltage window is beneficial to enhance the energy density of flow hybrid supercapacitor.

The charge and discharge curves and specific capacity of flow hybrid supercapacitor at different current densities with the potential window of 1.65 V were measured in 3 M KOH aqueous solution. Fig. 11 shows the each of discharge curve can be divided into three stages. The stage at the potential range from 1.65 to 1.4 V and the stage at the potential range from 0.5 to 0 V indicate fast voltage decrease process. The two stages produce EDLC. The stage at the potential range from 1.4 to 0.5 V is obviously deviated from straight line, verifying that the stage mainly produces pseudo capacity. The pseudo capacity is mainly attributed to the oxidation and reduction of NiCo-GQD-G. Fig. 11B indicates that the capacity of NiCo-GQD-G electrode will slowly reduce with the increase of current density. This is because the electron transfer rate is faster than that of electrochemical reaction. Thus, the electrode material has no enough time to take part in the redox reaction and leads to a decreased capacity. The capacity of NiCo-GQD-G electrode is 380 F g−1 at the current density of 20 A g−1 and 325 F g−1 at the current density of 50 A g−1. These data verify that the NiCo-GQD-G electrode gives good high-rate performance. As far as I know, main problems affecting the application of flow hybrid supercapacitor are the contact between the current collector and the electrode material and electrical conductivity of electrode material. High superparamagnetic and small particle size make NiCo-GQD-G can be firmly adsorbed on the surface of nickel foam by magnetic force. As NiCo-GQD-G offers high electrical conductivity, the above contact helps to realize a fast electron transfer between current collector and NiCo-GQD-G. The fast electron transfer leads to a good high-rate performance.

Energy density and power density of NiCo-GQD-G electrode in 3 M KOH aqueous electrolyte were obtained accroding to the reported equation [43]. Fig. 12 shows that the energy density reaches 247.4 W h kg−1 at power density of 6596 W kg−1 and 111.2 W h kg−1 at power density of 160100 W kg−1. The above energy densities are better than that of the NiO/graphene [44], CoS/NiO [45], NiO–CoO/graphene [46], CoO/Co3O4 [47], CoO nanowall [48], snowflake-like CoO [49], CoO/graphene [50], carbon-encapsulated Co3O4@CoO@Co [51] and (Co–Ni)@(CoO, NiO) [52].

The study demonstrated controllable synthesis of superparamagnetic NiCo-graphene quantum dot-graphene composite. The use of histidine-functionalized graphene quantum dots achieves to in-situ formation, fixation and structural control of NiCo nanomaterials on graphene sheets. The as-synthesized composite exhibits good three-dimensional structure, monodispersity, catalytic activity and superparamagnetic. The NiCo nanocrystals in the composite were covered by NiO and CoO to form a core-shell structures with the average particle size of only several nanometers. The unique architecture creates excellent electrochemical properties, including high capacity, high-rate performance and cycle stability. The composite as a battery-type electrode material provides high energy density and power density for magnetic field-controlled electrochemical flow hybrid supercapacitor. The synthesis strategy can be widely applied in fabrication of various metal-graphene composites with excellent dispersion and controllable nanostructure that meets the needs of further applications in the future.
